c5f80097885c89be.summary: Trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan, cosec, sec, cot) relate angles of a right triangle to ratios of its sides. For angle A: sin A = opposite/hypotenuse, cos A = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan A = opposite/adjacent.

chunks.title: Understanding Trigonometric Ratios
chunks.content:
**Trigonometric Ratios** is a key concept in Introduction to Trigonometry of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

For a right triangle ABC with right angle at B:
• sin A = opposite/hypotenuse = BC/AC
• cos A = adjacent/hypotenuse = AB/AC
• tan A = opposite/adjacent = BC/AB
• cosec A = 1/sin A = AC/BC
• sec A = 1/cos A = AC/AB
• cot A = 1/tan A = AB/BC

**Key points to remember:**
• SOH CAH TOA: Sin=Opp/Hyp, Cos=Adj/Hyp, Tan=Opp/Adj
• Trigonometry (Greek: trigonon=triangle, metron=measure)
• All six ratios are defined for acute angles in right triangles
• The three reciprocal pairs: sin-cosec, cos-sec, tan-cot

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Trigonometric Ratios
chunks.content:
**1. Key Properties:**
• sin²A + cos²A = 1 (from Pythagoras theorem)
• tan A = sin A/cos A
• cot A = cos A/sin A
• Values range: sin, cos between 0 and 1 for acute angles

**2. Important Formulas:**
sin²A + cos²A = 1
sec²A = 1 + tan²A
cosec²A = 1 + cot²A

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
If AB=3, BC=4, AC=5 in right triangle ABC (right angle at B):
sin A = 4/5, cos A = 3/5, tan A = 4/3
Check: sin²A+cos²A = 16/25+9/25 = 1 ✓

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** In right triangle ABC right-angled at B, AB=7 cm, BC=24 cm. Find sin A, cos A, and tan A.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find AC: AC = √(49+576) = √625 = 25 cm
**Step 2:** sin A = opposite/hypotenuse = 24/25
**Step 3:** cos A = adjacent/hypotenuse = 7/25
**Step 4:** tan A = opposite/adjacent = 24/7

Therefore, sin A = 24/25, cos A = 7/25, tan A = 24/7.

**Verification:**
sin²A+cos²A = (576+49)/625 = 1 ✓

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Confusing opposite and adjacent:** Opposite is across from the angle; adjacent is next to it

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **SOH CAH TOA:** Sin=Opp/Hyp, Cos=Adj/Hyp, Tan=Opp/Adj

✓ **Draw and label:** Label the triangle clearly before computing

Mastering these will help you avoid errors and solve problems efficiently.

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
If sin A = 3/5, find cos A and tan A.

**Answer:** cos²A=1-9/25=16/25 → cos A=4/5. tan A=sin A/cos A=3/4.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
In triangle ABC right-angled at B, if tan A = √3, find sin A and cos A.

**Answer:** tan A = √3 = opp/adj. Let opp=√3k, adj=k. Hyp=√(3k²+k²)=2k. sin A=√3/2, cos A=1/2.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
If ∠A and ∠B are acute with cos A = cos B, prove ∠A = ∠B.

**Answer:** cos A = cos B implies adjacent/hypotenuse are equal. In a right triangle with common hypotenuse, this implies A = B = 45°.

5f0556a9da228d2a.summary: Trigonometric ratios for 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° have exact values derived from geometric constructions. The 45° values come from an isosceles right triangle; 30° and 60° values from an equilateral triangle.

chunks.title: Understanding Trigonometric Ratios of Some Specific Angles
chunks.content:
**Trigonometric Ratios of Some Specific Angles** is a key concept in Introduction to Trigonometry of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

The angles 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, and 90° have exact trigonometric values derived from simple geometric figures.

**Values Table:**
| Angle | 0° | 30° | 45° | 60° | 90° |
| sin | 0 | 1/2 | 1/√2 | √3/2 | 1 |
| cos | 1 | √3/2 | 1/√2 | 1/2 | 0 |
| tan | 0 | 1/√3 | 1 | √3 | ∞ |

**Key points to remember:**
• sin values follow pattern: √0/2, √1/2, √2/2, √3/2, √4/2
• cos values are sin in reverse order
• tan = sin/cos — derive if you forget
• 45° comes from isosceles right triangle with legs 1, hypotenuse √2

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Trigonometric Ratios of Specific Angles
chunks.content:
**1. Key Properties:**
• sin 0°=0, sin 90°=1 (boundary values)
• cos 0°=1, cos 90°=0
• tan 45°=1 (special value to remember)
• tan 90° is undefined (not 0 or 1)

**2. Important Formulas:**
sin 30° = 1/2, sin 45° = 1/√2, sin 60° = √3/2
cos 30° = √3/2, cos 45° = 1/√2, cos 60° = 1/2
tan 30° = 1/√3, tan 45° = 1, tan 60° = √3

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Find 2sin²30° - 3cos²45° + tan²60°
= 2(1/4) - 3(1/2) + 3 = 1/2 - 3/2 + 3 = 2

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Evaluate: (sin 30° + cos 45° - tan 60°) / (cot 45° + sec 30° - cosec 60°)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Values: sin 30°=1/2, cos 45°=1/√2, tan 60°=√3, cot 45°=1, sec 30°=2/√3, cosec 60°=2/√3
**Step 2:** Numerator = 1/2 + 1/√2 - √3
**Step 3:** Denominator = 1 + 2/√3 - 2/√3 = 1
**Step 4:** Expression = 1/2 + 1/√2 - √3

Therefore, the value is 1/2 + 1/√2 - √3.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Confusing sin and cos values:** sin 30°=1/2, sin 60°=√3/2 (smaller angle, smaller sine)

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **√ pattern:** sin: √0/2, √1/2, √2/2, √3/2, √4/2

✓ **Reverse for cos:** cos is sin in reverse order

Mastering these will help you avoid errors and solve problems efficiently.

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Find 2sin²30° - 3cos²45° + tan²60°.

**Answer:** 2(1/4)-3(1/2)+3 = 1/2-3/2+3 = 2.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
If sin(A+B)=1 and cos(A-B)=√3/2, find A and B (acute).

**Answer:** A+B=90°, A-B=30°. A=60°, B=30°.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Prove (sin 30°+cos 60°) - (sin 60°+cos 30°) = -(sin 45°+cos 45°).

**Answer:** LHS = (1/2+1/2)-(√3/2+√3/2) = 1-√3. RHS = -(1/√2+1/√2) = -√2. Not equal — this is false; it's an evaluation problem.

27973af099cc7581.summary: Complementary angle relations: sin(90°-A)=cos A, cos(90°-A)=sin A, tan(90°-A)=cot A, etc. These show sine and cosine (and other pairs) are complementary functions, simplifying trigonometric expressions.

chunks.title: Understanding Trigonometric Ratios of Complementary Angles
chunks.content:
**Trigonometric Ratios of Complementary Angles** is a key concept in Introduction to Trigonometry of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

Two angles are complementary if their sum is 90°. In a right triangle, the acute angles are complementary.

**Relations:**
sin(90°-A) = cos A
cos(90°-A) = sin A
tan(90°-A) = cot A
cot(90°-A) = tan A
sec(90°-A) = cosec A
cosec(90°-A) = sec A

**Memory aid:** 'co' in cosine/cosec/cot stands for 'complementary'.

**Key points to remember:**
• If A+B=90°, then sin A = cos B, tan A = cot B
• Useful for simplifying expressions
• Works both ways: sin(90°-A)=cos A AND cos A=sin(90°-A)
• Only for acute angles in Class X

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Complementary Angle Relations
chunks.content:
**1. Key Properties:**
• sin 30° = cos 60° (since 30°+60°=90°)
• tan 50° = cot 40°
• sec 70° = cosec 20°
• cos A = sin(90°-A) — always true

**2. Important Formulas:**
sin(90°-A)=cos A, cos(90°-A)=sin A
tan(90°-A)=cot A, cot(90°-A)=tan A
sec(90°-A)=cosec A, cosec(90°-A)=sec A

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Evaluate tan 35°/cot 55° + cot 78°/tan 12°
cot 55° = tan(90°-55°) = tan 35°, so first term = 1
cot 78° = tan(90°-78°) = tan 12°, so second term = 1
Sum = 2

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** If sin 3A = cos(A-26°), where 3A is acute, find A.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** sin 3A = cos(90°-3A) (using complementary relation)
**Step 2:** cos(90°-3A) = cos(A-26°)
**Step 3:** So 90°-3A = A-26°
**Step 4:** 90°+26° = A+3A → 116° = 4A → A = 29°
**Step 5:** Verification: sin 87° = cos 3° and cos(29°-26°) = cos 3° ✓

Therefore, A = 29°.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Confusing complementary (90°) with supplementary (180°):** These are different concepts

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **The 'co' trick:** cosine = sine of complementary angle

✓ **Quick conversion:** sin↔cos, tan↔cot, sec↔cosec

Mastering these will help you avoid errors and solve problems efficiently.

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Evaluate: tan 35°/cot 55° + cot 78°/tan 12°

**Answer:** cot 55°=tan 35°, so 1st=1. cot 78°=tan 12°, so 2nd=1. Sum=2.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
If tan 2A = cot(A-18°), find A.

**Answer:** tan 2A = cot(90°-2A) = cot(A-18°) → 90°-2A = A-18° → 108°=3A → A=36°.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Prove sin(90°-θ)cos(90°-θ) = tan θ/(1+tan²θ).

**Answer:** LHS = cosθ·sinθ. RHS = (sinθ/cosθ)/sec²θ = (sinθ/cosθ)cos²θ = sinθ cosθ = LHS ✓.

44aea0e51ce5c4af.summary: The three fundamental trigonometric identities: sin²A+cos²A=1, sec²A=1+tan²A, cosec²A=1+cot²A. These are used to simplify expressions and prove other trigonometric relationships.

chunks.title: Understanding Trigonometric Identities
chunks.content:
**Trigonometric Identities** is a key concept in Introduction to Trigonometry of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

A trigonometric identity is an equation involving trigonometric ratios that is true for all values for which the ratios are defined.

**Three Fundamental Identities:**
1. sin²A + cos²A = 1
2. sec²A = 1 + tan²A
3. cosec²A = 1 + cot²A

**Derivation:** Identity 1 comes from Pythagoras theorem. Dividing by cos²A gives Identity 2. Dividing by sin²A gives Identity 3.

**Key points to remember:**
• sin²A means (sin A)², NOT sin(A²)
• Identity 1 works for all angles
• Identities 2 and 3 have domain restrictions
• Use these to prove other identities

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Trigonometric Identities
chunks.content:
**1. Key Properties:**
• sin²A + cos²A = 1 → sin²A = 1-cos²A, cos²A = 1-sin²A
• sec²A = 1+tan²A → sec²A-tan²A = 1
• cosec²A = 1+cot²A → cosec²A-cot²A = 1
• Convert to sin and cos when stuck

**2. Important Formulas:**
sin²A + cos²A = 1
sec²A = 1 + tan²A
cosec²A = 1 + cot²A

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Prove (cosec A - sin A)(sec A - cos A) = 1/(tan A + cot A)
LHS = (1/sinA-sinA)(1/cosA-cosA) = (cos²A/sinA)(sin²A/cosA) = cosA sinA
RHS = 1/(sinA/cosA+cosA/sinA) = 1/((sin²A+cos²A)/(sinA cosA)) = sinA cosA
LHS = RHS ✓

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Prove (cosec A - sin A)(sec A - cos A) = 1/(tan A + cot A)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** LHS = (1/sin A - sin A)(1/cos A - cos A)
**Step 2:** = ((1-sin²A)/sin A)((1-cos²A)/cos A)
**Step 3:** = (cos²A/sin A)(sin²A/cos A) = cos A sin A
**Step 4:** RHS = 1/(sin A/cos A + cos A/sin A)
**Step 5:** = 1/((sin²A+cos²A)/(sin A cos A)) = sin A cos A
**Step 6:** LHS = RHS ✓

Therefore, the identity is proved.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **sin²A vs sin(A²):** sin²A = (sin A)², not sin(A²)

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Convert to sin and cos:** This usually simplifies the expression

✓ **Start with complex side:** Simplify the more complicated side first

Mastering these will help you avoid errors and solve problems efficiently.

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Prove (1+cos A)(1-cos A) = sin²A.

**Answer:** LHS = 1-cos²A = sin²A = RHS ✓.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Prove (sec A+tan A)(sec A-tan A) = 1.

**Answer:** LHS = sec²A-tan²A = 1 = RHS ✓ (using sec²A = 1+tan²A).

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Prove √((1+sin A)/(1-sin A)) = sec A + tan A.

**Answer:** LHS = √((1+sinA)²/(1-sin²A)) = (1+sinA)/|cosA|. For acute A, cosA>0, so = (1+sinA)/cosA = secA+tanA = RHS ✓.

dce12fac6c378302.summary: Heights and distances problems use trigonometric ratios to measure inaccessible heights. The angle of elevation (looking up) or depression (looking down) forms a right triangle with the height and distance.

chunks.title: Understanding Heights and Distances
chunks.content:
**Heights and Distances** is a key concept in Some Applications of Trigonometry of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Angle of Elevation:** The angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when the object is above the observer (looking UP).
**Angle of Depression:** The angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when the object is below the observer (looking DOWN).

**Key Fact:** Angle of elevation from A to B = Angle of depression from B to A (alternate interior angles).

**Basic Setup:**
tan θ = height/distance
sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse
cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse

**Key points to remember:**
• Draw a clear diagram first
• Use tan when relating height and distance
• Angle of elevation = angle of depression (from other side)
• Common angles: 30°, 45°, 60° have exact values

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Heights and Distances
chunks.content:
**1. Key Properties:**
• tan θ = height of object / distance from object
• sin θ = height / line-of-sight length
• For two-position problems, set up two equations
• Reject negative/non-realistic answers

**2. Important Formulas:**
tan θ = opposite/adjacent = height/distance
sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse
cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
A tower casts a shadow 15 m long when sun's altitude is 30°.
tan 30° = h/15 → 1/√3 = h/15 → h = 15/√3 = 5√3 ≈ 8.66 m

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** The angle of elevation of the top of a building from a point is 45°. On moving 20 m closer, the angle becomes 60°. Find the height.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Let height = h m, initial distance = d m
**Step 2:** First: tan 45° = h/d → h = d
**Step 3:** Second: tan 60° = h/(d-20) → h = √3(d-20)
**Step 4:** d = √3(d-20) → d = √3d - 20√3 → 20√3 = d(√3-1)
**Step 5:** d = 20√3/(√3-1) = 10(3+√3) ≈ 47.32 m
**Step 6:** h = d = 10(3+√3) ≈ 47.32 m

Therefore, the height is 10(3+√3) ≈ 47.32 m.

**Verification:**
At 47.32 m, tan=1 ✓. At 27.32 m, tan=47.32/27.32≈√3 ✓

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Elevation vs Depression:** Elevation = looking UP, Depression = looking DOWN

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Draw a diagram first:** Essential for understanding the geometry

✓ **Angle equality:** Angle of elevation = angle of depression (alternate angles)

Mastering these will help you avoid errors and solve problems efficiently.

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
A pole casts shadow 15 m long when sun's altitude is 30°. Find pole height.

**Answer:** tan 30° = h/15 → h = 15/√3 = 5√3 ≈ 8.66 m.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
A kite flies at 60 m height with string making 60° angle with ground. Find string length.

**Answer:** sin 60° = 60/L → L = 120/√3 = 40√3 ≈ 69.28 m.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
From a 45 m lighthouse, depression angles of two ships are 30° and 45°. Find distance between ships.

**Answer:** tan 45°=45/d₁ → d₁=45. tan 30°=45/d₂ → d₂=45√3. Distance = 45(√3-1) ≈ 32.94 m.

ae71fff4c23c7719.summary: A tangent touches a circle at exactly one point. The radius to the point of contact is perpendicular to the tangent. From an external point, two tangents of equal length can be drawn.

chunks.title: Understanding Tangent to a Circle
chunks.content:
**Tangent to a Circle** is a key concept in Circles of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

A **tangent** is a line that touches a circle at exactly one point (the point of contact).

**Key Property:** The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

A **secant** intersects the circle at two points. A tangent is a secant where the two intersection points coincide.

**Key points to remember:**
• Tangent ⟂ radius at point of contact
• Exactly one tangent at any point on the circle
• From an external point: TWO tangents of equal length
• Use Pythagoras to find tangent length

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Tangent to a Circle
chunks.content:
**1. Key Properties:**
• Radius to point of contact ⟂ tangent
• Tangents from same external point are equal
• The line joining centre to external point bisects the angle between tangents
• Quadrilateral formed by centre, two points of contact, and external point has sum 360°

**2. Important Formulas:**
PA = PB (tangents from external point P)
PA² = OP² - r² (Pythagoras, where O=centre, r=radius)
sin(θ/2) = r/OP (θ = angle between tangents)

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Find tangent length from point 10 cm away from centre of circle radius 6 cm.
PT = √(100-36) = √64 = 8 cm.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A tangent PQ at point P of a circle of radius 5 cm meets a line through centre O at Q such that OQ=13 cm. Find PQ.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Given: OP=5 cm (radius), OQ=13 cm
**Step 2:** Tangent ⟂ radius → ∠OPQ = 90°
**Step 3:** By Pythagoras: PQ² = OQ²-OP²
**Step 4:** = 169-25 = 144
**Step 5:** PQ = √144 = 12 cm

Therefore, PQ = 12 cm.

**Verification:**
OP²+PQ² = 25+144 = 169 = 13² ✓

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Tangent vs secant:** Tangent touches at 1 point; secant intersects at 2 points

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Mark right angle:** Tangent ⟂ radius creates a right triangle

✓ **Two equal tangents:** From external point, two tangents of equal length

Mastering these will help you avoid errors and solve problems efficiently.

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Find tangent length from point 17 cm away from centre of circle radius 8 cm.

**Answer:** PT = √(289-64) = √225 = 15 cm.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Two tangents TP, TQ are drawn to a circle from external point T. Prove ∠PTQ = 2∠OPQ.

**Answer:** In quadrilateral OPTQ: ∠OPT=∠OQT=90°. Sum=360° → ∠POQ+∠PTQ=180°. In △OPQ: OP=OQ, so ∠OPQ=∠OQP. ∠POQ+2∠OPQ=180°. Therefore ∠PTQ=2∠OPQ.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Find tangent length from point 25 cm from centre of circle radius 7 cm.

**Answer:** PT = √(625-49) = √576 = 24 cm.

39c510c9af0e165f.summary: From an external point, exactly two tangents of equal length can be drawn. From a point on the circle, one tangent. From inside, no tangents. The angle between tangents is supplementary to the central angle.

chunks.title: Understanding Number of Tangents from a Point on a Circle
chunks.content:
**Number of Tangents from a Point on a Circle** is a key concept in Circles of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

The number of tangents depends on the point's position:
• **Inside** the circle: ZERO tangents
• **On** the circle: EXACTLY ONE tangent
• **Outside** the circle: EXACTLY TWO tangents (equal length)

**For two tangents from external point P:**
PA = PB (lengths equal)
∠AOB + ∠APB = 180° (supplementary)
OP bisects ∠APB and ∠AOB

**Key points to remember:**
• PA = PB = √(OP²-r²)
• PA ⟂ OA and PB ⟂ OB
• ∠AOB + ∠APB = 180°
• As P moves farther, angle between tangents decreases

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Number of Tangents from a Point
chunks.content:
**1. Key Properties:**
• Point inside: 0 tangents
• Point on circle: 1 tangent (perp. to radius)
• Point outside: 2 tangents (equal length)
• Tangent length = √(OP²-r²)

**2. Important Formulas:**
PA = PB = √(OP²-r²)
sin(θ/2) = r/OP (θ = ∠APB)
∠AOB + ∠APB = 180°

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Two tangents from P to circle radius 5 cm. OP=13 cm.
PA = √(169-25) = √144 = 12 cm
sin(θ/2) = 5/13 → θ/2 = sin⁻¹(5/13)

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** From external point P, two tangents PT, PS are drawn to a circle with centre O. OP=17 cm, radius=8 cm. Find PT and the angle between tangents.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** In right triangle OTP: PT² = OP²-OT² = 289-64 = 225
**Step 2:** PT = √225 = 15 cm
**Step 3:** Since PS=PT: PS = 15 cm
**Step 4:** sin(∠OPT) = OT/OP = 8/17
**Step 5:** ∠TPS = 2×∠OPT = 2 sin⁻¹(8/17)

Therefore, PT = PS = 15 cm, angle = 2 sin⁻¹(8/17).

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Inside vs outside:** From inside: no tangents. From outside: two tangents.

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Count check:** Inside(0), On(1), Outside(2)

✓ **Supplementary:** ∠AOB+∠APB=180° — useful for finding angles

Mastering these will help you avoid errors and solve problems efficiently.

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
A point P is 10 cm from centre of circle radius 6 cm. Find tangent length.

**Answer:** PT = √(100-36) = √64 = 8 cm.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Two concentric circles have radii 5 cm and 3 cm. Find chord length of larger circle touching the smaller.

**Answer:** Half-chord = √(25-9) = 4 cm. Full chord = 8 cm.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Prove angle between tangents from external point is supplementary to the central angle.

**Answer:** In quadrilateral OAPB: ∠OAP=∠OBP=90°. Sum=360° → ∠AOB+90°+90°+∠APB=360° → ∠AOB+∠APB=180°.
